parsed.summary: The v0.4.0→v0.4.6 cumulative diff introduces new retry logic, BigModel error dispatch, job tracking, and state management without sufficient evidence of idempotency, race-condition safety, or atomic state transitions. Multiple critical paths appear vulnerable to partial-failure corruption and silent data loss. I cannot confirm this is safe to ship without direct code inspection of the highest-risk modules.

findings.title: retry.mjs may lack idempotency guards for non-retryable side effects
findings.body: A new 203-line retry module (scripts/lib/retry.mjs) is the backbone of the v0.4.6 auto-retry/backoff feature. If the retried operation has already partially committed side effects (e.g., writing state, dispatching a job) before the failure that triggers a retry, the retry will re-execute the entire operation causing duplication or corruption. Without explicit idempotency tokens or deduplication keys visible in the diff stat, this is a classic partial-failure hazard. The 352-line test file (tests/retry.test.mjs) may cover happy-path retry but likely does not exercise the partial-commit-then-retry scenario.
findings.recommendation: Audit retry.mjs for operations that are not safe to replay. Either (a) make the retried function idempotent by design, (b) add a deduplication key passed to the upstream, or (c) document explicitly which operations are safe to retry and guard the others. Add a test case that simulates a partial-commit failure followed by retry to verify no duplication occurs.

findings.title: State transitions in state.mjs and tracked-jobs.mjs may not be atomic under concurrent access
findings.body: Both scripts/lib/state.mjs (32 lines changed) and scripts/lib/tracked-jobs.mjs (78 lines changed) manage persisted job/state data. If a user runs multiple glm commands concurrently (e.g., two review invocations), there is no visible file-locking or atomic write-and-rename pattern in the diff stat. The most common failure mode is: process A reads state, process B reads same state, both write their modified version, and one update is silently lost. This is a data-loss risk for tracked jobs.
findings.recommendation: Verify that state.mjs and tracked-jobs.mjs use atomic file writes (write-to-temp-then-rename). If concurrent access is possible, add file-level locking (e.g., proper-lockfile) or serialize via a lock file. Add a concurrent-access test that writes state from two processes simultaneously and verifies no data loss.

findings.title: bigmodel-errors.mjs error classification could silently swallow unknown errors
findings.body: The new 168-line scripts/lib/bigmodel-errors.mjs module classifies BigModel API errors for dispatch and retry decisions. If an error does not match any known classification, the default behavior must be to propagate it, not to treat it as retryable or ignorable. Misclassification leads to either infinite retry loops (if unknown errors are treated as retryable) or silent failures (if unknown errors are swallowed). The test file (tests/bigmodel-errors.test.mjs, 189 lines) should explicitly test the unclassifiable-error path.
findings.recommendation: Verify the default/fallback branch in error classification: unknown errors must propagate as non-retryable. Add an explicit test for an error with an unrecognized code/structure to confirm it is not swallowed and is not retried.

findings.title: glm-client.mjs changes may leak credentials or tokens in error output
findings.body: scripts/lib/glm-client.mjs has 155 lines changed and is the HTTP client that talks to the BigModel API. Error handling that includes request details (headers, URL, body) could leak API keys or session tokens into logs, stderr, or state files. This is an auth/trust-boundary concern. The retry integration also means failed requests are replayed — if the first attempt's error response includes sensitive data that gets cached for retry logic, that data persists longer than intended.
findings.recommendation: Audit all error objects and log messages in glm-client.mjs for inclusion of Authorization headers, API keys, or tokens. Ensure error sanitization before any persistent storage or user-visible output. Add a test that verifies no auth token appears in error messages or log output.

findings.title: Missing rollback path for session-lifecycle-hook.mjs changes
findings.body: scripts/session-lifecycle-hook.mjs has 5 lines changed. Lifecycle hooks execute at session boundaries where partial failure is most dangerous — if the hook fails mid-execution, the session may be in an inconsistent state with no rollback. Given the new retry and job-tracking features, a hook failure could leave tracked jobs in a state that never gets cleaned up.
findings.recommendation: Verify session-lifecycle-hook.mjs has a try/catch that either rolls back partial state changes or leaves the system in a known-safe state on failure. Add a test that simulates a mid-hook failure and verifies no orphaned state remains.

next_steps: Read retry.mjs fully to confirm whether retried operations are idempotent and whether partial-commit scenarios are handled.
Read state.mjs and tracked-jobs.mjs to verify atomic write patterns and concurrent-access safety.
Read bigmodel-errors.mjs default/fallback error path to confirm unknown errors propagate correctly.
Read glm-client.mjs error handling paths to verify no credential leakage.
Read session-lifecycle-hook.mjs to verify failure safety and cleanup paths.
